label: Post-fall neuro check — Room 422
text: Post-fall assessment, room 422, twenty-one fifteen. Patient found sitting on the floor beside the bed, denies hitting her head, no visible trauma. Eyes are open spontaneously — she was looking at me when I walked in. She's answering all my questions appropriately. Knows her name, knows she's in the hospital, knows it's July. Fully oriented. Follows commands — squeezed both my hands on request, wiggled her toes. So eyes four, verbal five, motor six. Glasgow fifteen. Pupils equal, 4 millimeters, brisk both sides. Face symmetric. Speech clear. Strength equal and strong, all four extremities. No seizure activity. Will continue neuro checks per fall protocol.

label: Ambiguous arousal — Room 418
text: Neuro check, room 418, oh-two-hundred. She was pretty drowsy when I came in. Eyes were closed — I said her name and she opened them, though honestly she may have been starting to wake up on her own around then, hard to say. Speech was a little thick but she told me her name. Didn't quite track the date. Moved her right arm to my hand but the left just kind of pulled back. Pupils three millimeters, equal, react. No facial droop that I could see. No seizures.

label: Stroke alert — NIH Stroke Scale — Bed 3
text: Stroke alert, bed 3. Seventy-two-year-old woman, last known well ninety minutes ago, sudden left-sided weakness and facial droop. Running the NIH Stroke Scale now. She's alert and answering. She told me the month and her age, both correct. She opens and closes her eyes on command and grips and releases my hand on command. Her eyes show a partial gaze palsy — she doesn't fully cross midline to the left. Visual fields, she has a complete left homonymous hemianopia. There's a clear left lower facial droop, partial paralysis. Her left arm drifts down before ten seconds. The right arm holds with no drift. Her left leg has some effort against gravity but drops to the bed. The right leg holds. No limb ataxia. Decreased sensation on the left to pinprick, mild to moderate. She has mild expressive aphasia with some word-finding trouble. Her speech is mildly slurred, dysarthria is present but intelligible. And she's ignoring things on her left side — clear left-sided inattention.

label: Comprehensive stroke code — Room 415 (tests everything)
text: Stroke code, room 415, fourteen-thirty. Sixty-eight-year-old woman, last known well two hours ago, acute left-sided weakness and facial droop, family called it in. Doing a full neuro assessment. Her eyes are open spontaneously. She's confused — she knew her age but couldn't tell me the month, and her words are jumbled. She does follow commands, squeezed my hand and stuck out her tongue on request. So on the coma scale that's eyes four, verbal four, motor six — Glasgow fourteen. Her pupils are three millimeters, equal and briskly reactive. There's an obvious left facial droop. She has clear expressive aphasia, real word-finding trouble. Left-sided weakness throughout. No seizure activity. Now the NIH Stroke Scale. Level of consciousness, she's alert. For the questions she got her age right but not the month, so one correct. Commands, she did both — eyes and grip. Her gaze shows a partial palsy, doesn't fully cross to the left. Visual fields, complete left homonymous hemianopia. Facial, partial lower-face droop. Left arm, she gets it up but it drifts down with some effort against gravity. Right arm holds fine. Left leg drifts but keeps some effort. Right leg is normal. I honestly couldn't reliably test for limb ataxia given how weak that side is — not sure whether to call it absent or untestable. Sensation is reduced on the left, mild to moderate. Language, mild to moderate aphasia. Her speech is mildly slurred, dysarthria present. And she's clearly neglecting her left side, inattention.
